tf.unique returns (y, idx) where y is 1-D unique elements, while torch.unique by default returns only the unique elements tensor; to match output structure and shape, PyTorch must set return_inverse=False (default) but TF's idx is always returned — however the shape mismatch (TF=(2,7000) vs PT=(7000,)) indicates TF test incorrectly interpreted output as stacked tuple; actual tf.unique returns two separate tensors, so comparison must unpack them. PyTorch test must explicitly request only the unique values (no inverse/counts) and ensure same output semantics: use torch.unique(input, sorted=True, return_inverse=False, return_counts=False). Also, string dtype is supported in both, but torch.unique requires input to be 1-D and doesn't return idx — so to compare 'y' only, PT case is correct, but TF result was likely misread as concatenated shape; repair by aligning to return only unique values (first output of tf.unique, and sole output of torch.unique).

TensorFlow tf.unique returns (y, idx) tuple where y is 1-D unique values; PyTorch torch.unique by default returns only the unique values tensor, but the shape mismatch (TF=(2,7000) vs PT=(7000,)) indicates TF output was misinterpreted — tf.unique actually returns y (1-D) and idx (1-D), so the comparison likely incorrectly treated the tuple as a 2D tensor. To align semantics, PyTorch must be configured to return only the unique values (no inverse/counts), and TF output must be compared only on y. However, the reported shapes suggest test logic error: tf.unique never outputs (2,7000); it outputs two 1-D tensors. Thus, repair is needed to ensure correct extraction and comparison.

tf.unique returns (y, idx) tuple with y.shape=[num_unique], while torch.unique by default returns only the unique values tensor; to match TF's output structure and shape semantics, PT must set return_inverse=True to get comparable idx, but here shape mismatch (2,7000) vs (6998,) suggests TF returned tuple and PT returned single tensor — repair by aligning return behavior: TF outputs two tensors, so PT must return at least output + inverse_indices to enable fair comparison of first output.
